nameJa: 動詞のグループと活用

sections.title: る-Verbs (Group 2 / Ichidan)
sections.explanation: る-verbs (also called Group 2 or ichidan verbs) are the easiest to conjugate. They always end in -いる (-iru) or -える (-eru) in dictionary form. To conjugate, simply drop the final る and add the new ending. Examples: 食べる (taberu, to eat) → 食べます (tabemasu, polite), 見る (miru, to see) → 見ます (mimasu, polite). Common る-verbs: 食べる (eat), 見る (see), 寝る (sleep), 起きる (wake up), 出る (leave), 着る (wear), いる (exist, animate), 教える (teach), 開ける (open), 閉める (close). The conjugation is completely regular — once you know how to conjugate one る-verb, you know them all. The stem (what remains after dropping る) never changes.
sections.tip: If a verb ends in -iru or -eru, it is PROBABLY a る-verb, but there are exceptions! 帰る (kaeru, to return), 走る (hashiru, to run), 入る (hairu, to enter), and 切る (kiru, to cut) end in -iru/-eru but are actually う-verbs. You need to memorize these exceptions.

examples.en: taberu → tabemasu (to eat → polite form: drop る, add ます)
okiru → okimasu (to wake up → polite form)

exercises.prompt: How do you conjugate る-verbs to ます form?
options: Drop る and add ます
Change る to り and add ます
Add ます directly after る
exercises.explanation: For る-verbs, simply drop the final る and add ます: 食べる → 食べ + ます = 食べます. The stem never changes.

exercises.prompt: What is the ます form of 寝る (neru, to sleep)?
exercises.explanation: 寝る is a る-verb: drop る → 寝, then add ます → 寝ます (nemasu).

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a る-verb?
exercises.explanation: 食べる is a genuine る-verb (ends in -eru, conjugates as 食べ+ます). 帰る and 走る look like る-verbs but are actually う-verbs (exceptions that must be memorized).

sections.title: う-Verbs (Group 1 / Godan)
sections.explanation: う-verbs (also called Group 1 or godan verbs) end in an -u sound in dictionary form: -う, -く, -ぐ, -す, -つ, -ぬ, -ぶ, -む, -る. To make the ます form, change the final -u syllable to the corresponding -i syllable, then add ます. The mappings are: う→い, く→き, ぐ→ぎ, す→し, つ→ち, ぬ→に, ぶ→び, む→み, る→り. Examples: 書く (kaku, write) → 書きます (kakimasu), 飲む (nomu, drink) → 飲みます (nomimasu), 話す (hanasu, speak) → 話します (hanashimasu), 買う (kau, buy) → 買います (kaimasu). These are the largest group of Japanese verbs.
sections.tip: The key pattern: change the last vowel from 'u' to 'i' before adding ます. Think of the あいうえお column — you are moving from the う-row to the い-row: く→き, む→み, す→し, etc. For verbs ending in う, insert い: 買う → 買います (ka-u → ka-i-masu).

examples.en: kaku → kakimasu (to write: く→き + ます)
nomu → nomimasu (to drink: む→み + ます)
hanasu → hanashimasu (to speak: す→し + ます)
matsu → machimasu (to wait: つ→ち + ます)

exercises.prompt: What is the ます form of 読む (yomu, to read)?
exercises.explanation: 読む is an う-verb: change む to み, then add ます → 読みます (yomimasu). The む→み change follows the u-row to i-row pattern.

exercises.prompt: What is the ます form of 買う (kau, to buy)?
exercises.explanation: For verbs ending in う, change う to い: 買う → 買い, then add ます → 買います (kaimasu).

exercises.prompt: How do you convert an う-verb's ending to make the ます form?
options: Change the final -u syllable to its -i counterpart
exercises.explanation: The rule for う-verbs: shift the final syllable from the u-column to the i-column (く→き, む→み, す→し, etc.), then add ます.

sections.title: Irregular Verbs: する and 来る
sections.explanation: Japanese has only two truly irregular verbs: する (suru, to do) and 来る (kuru, to come). These do not follow the patterns of either る-verbs or う-verbs and must be memorized individually. する conjugations: します (shimasu, polite), しない (shinai, negative), した (shita, past), して (shite, te-form). 来る conjugations: 来ます (kimasu, polite), 来ない (konai, negative), 来た (kita, past), 来て (kite, te-form). Notice 来る is unusual because the kanji 来 changes pronunciation depending on the form: くる (kuru), きます (kimasu), こない (konai). する is extremely common because it combines with nouns to create compound verbs: 勉強する (benkyō suru, to study), 料理する (ryōri suru, to cook), 運動する (undō suru, to exercise), 旅行する (ryokō suru, to travel).
sections.tip: The good news: there are only TWO irregular verbs in all of Japanese! Memorize する and 来る and their forms. Pay special attention to 来る — the reading of 来 changes: く (kuru), き (kimasu), こ (konai). Also learn the noun+する pattern, as it creates hundreds of useful verbs.

examples.ja: 勉強する → 勉強します
examples.en: benkyō suru → benkyō shimasu (to study → polite form)
kuru → kimasu (to come → polite form — note: く changes to き)
A friend will come tomorrow. (来ます = polite form of 来る)
I exercise every day. (運動する = noun + する compound verb)

exercises.prompt: How many truly irregular verbs does Japanese have?
exercises.explanation: Japanese has only two irregular verbs: する (to do) and 来る (to come). All other verbs follow either the る-verb or う-verb conjugation patterns.

exercises.prompt: What is the polite (ます) form of 来る (kuru)?
exercises.explanation: 来る → 来ます (kimasu). The reading changes from く (ku) to き (ki). This is irregular and must be memorized.

exercises.prompt: How does 勉強する (to study) become polite?
exercises.explanation: Noun+する compound verbs follow する's conjugation: 勉強する → 勉強します. The noun part stays the same; only する changes to します.

sections.title: ます Form Conjugation Summary
sections.explanation: The ます form is the polite present/future tense. Once you have the ます stem, you can create four polite forms. Affirmative present: 食べます (tabemasu — I eat / I will eat). Negative present: 食べません (tabemasen — I do not eat). Affirmative past: 食べました (tabemashita — I ate). Negative past: 食べませんでした (tabemasen deshita — I did not eat). These four forms work identically for all verb groups — once you reach the ます stem, the endings are completely regular. The present tense in Japanese covers both present habitual actions and future actions: 毎日食べます (I eat every day — habitual) and 明日食べます (I will eat tomorrow — future). Context and time words distinguish them.
sections.tip: Memorize this pattern: ます (present+), ません (present-), ました (past+), ませんでした (past-). This works for every single verb once you have the ます stem. Four endings, no exceptions.

examples.ja: 行きます / 行きません
examples.en: ikimasu / ikimasen (I go / I do not go)

examples.ja: 行きました / 行きませんでした
examples.en: ikimashita / ikimasen deshita (I went / I did not go)

examples.ja: 毎朝コーヒーを飲みます。
examples.en: I drink coffee every morning. (present habitual)

examples.ja: 昨日は何も食べませんでした。
examples.en: I did not eat anything yesterday. (negative past)

exercises.prompt: What is the polite negative past of 飲みます (nomimasu)?
options: 飲みませんでした (nomimasen deshita)
exercises.explanation: Polite negative past: ます stem + ませんでした. 飲み + ませんでした = 飲みませんでした (I did not drink).

exercises.prompt: What does 明日行きます mean?
options: I will go tomorrow. (ます covers future actions)
exercises.explanation: The ます form covers both present habitual and future actions. With a future time word like 明日 (tomorrow), 行きます means 'I will go'.

exercises.prompt: How many polite conjugation endings does the ます system have?
options: 4: ます, ません, ました, ませんでした
exercises.explanation: There are four ます-form endings: ます (present affirmative), ません (present negative), ました (past affirmative), ませんでした (past negative). These work for all verbs.
